description: This figure presents the Charpy impact toughness characteristics of an Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at three temperatures: -196°C, -77°C, and room temperature (RT). Subfigure (a) displays the impact load versus deflection curves, revealing that the maximum impact load increases with decreasing temperature (reaching approximately 31 kN at -196°C compared to 27 kN at RT), while the total deflection at fracture decreases significantly from over 20 mm at RT to less than 10 mm at -196°C, indicating a transition from ductile to more brittle fracture behavior. Subfigure (b) quantifies the total impact absorbed energy and its decomposition into three components: crack initiation energy (W_initiation), stable crack propagation energy (W_stable), and unstable crack propagation energy (W_unstable). The total absorbed energy decreases markedly from 327 J at RT to 277 J at -77°C and further to 188 J at -196°C. Notably, the stable propagation energy component shows the strongest temperature dependence, dropping from approximately 200 J at both RT and -77°C to only about 40 J at -196°C, while the initiation energy remains relatively constant across the temperature range. These results demonstrate that decreasing temperature severely degrades the impact toughness of this steel, primarily by suppressing stable crack propagation mechanisms rather than affecting crack initiation.
